disclaimer: This dashboard is a decision-support tool based on open-source intelligence (OSINT). It is NOT official guidance. Consult your embassy and local authorities for evacuation decisions.

sections.title: Air Defense / Interceptor Effectiveness
sections.summary: Day 35: 18 ballistic + 4 cruise + 47 drones engaged. Deaths 12 (unchanged). Injuries 203 (+12, biggest single-day jump of the ENTIRE WAR). Three weapon types testing all defense layers simultaneously. Injury surge suggests some getting through.
sections.analysis: The defense is working. Zero deaths in 4 days, zero injuries on Day 15. MOD opacity on intercept numbers is a reporting change, not necessarily a performance issue. Debris from successful intercepts remains the primary civilian risk.

sections.title: Attack Volume on UAE
sections.summary: Day 35: 69 total (18 ballistic + 4 cruise + 47 drones). HIGHEST SINCE DAY 7. All three weapon types deployed simultaneously for first time in 31 days. Week 5 avg now ~51/day, back to Week 2 levels. The decline has fully reversed.
sections.analysis: Volume collapse is sustained and real. KSA allocation increasing but from a small base. Iran's port rhetoric (Mar 14) did not translate into increased attacks on Day 15. Watch for any volume spike but current trend is clearly downward.

sections.title: Airport & Airspace Operations
sections.summary: DXB gradually resuming flights to selected destinations. Standard entry rules back in effect Apr 1. Emirates rebooking covers Feb 28-Apr 30. SalamAir suspended flights to Kuwait/Sharjah/Doha through Apr 15. BA cancellations to Dubai through May 31. Singapore Airlines to Dubai cancelled through May 31. Operational but far below normal.
sections.analysis: DXB fuel tank fire from drone debris triggered full flight suspension. Previous incidents (3 drone/debris events) did not stop operations. This one did. Unknown how long suspension will last — depends on fire containment and fuel infrastructure damage assessment. This validates the amber-to-red trigger for DXB that was in the original framework.

sections.title: Ports & Cargo Continuity
sections.summary: Hormuz partially reopening. Iran allowing 'non-hostile' ships + Philippine-flagged vessels (Apr 2). Bloomberg: 3 ships (2 oil supertankers + 1 LNG) transited via new Oman coast route Apr 2. Iran allowed fertilizer shipments. Still far from normal but selective transits increasing.
sections.analysis: Iran's port warning is real and reported by tier-1 sources, but rhetoric hasn't translated to kinetic action against ports. The Fujairah fire was debris from a successful intercept — not deliberate targeting. Alternative supply routes (Fujairah, Khorfakkan, Oman Green Corridor) mitigate port disruption risk. Hormuz showing first cracks with selective transits. Watch for any deliberate port targeting — that would be a genuine red trigger.

sections.title: Retail Prices & Consumer Stress
sections.summary: UAE Economy Minister: no signs of food shortages, urges responsible shopping. 140 new local food products in pipeline. Emirates Food Security Council framework holding. Global fertilizer crisis looming (50% of traded urea via Hormuz) but no visible UAE shortages yet.
sections.analysis: Green. Two weeks in, supply chain holding. Government response coordinated and aggressive on price gouging. Alternative routes (Fujairah, Khorfakkan, Oman Green Corridor) specifically built to maintain supply. Monitor if situation changes but current evidence = no stress.

sections.title: Oman Border & Muscat Viability
sections.summary: Oman route remains operational. SalamAir selling tickets at UAE-Oman land border crossings for stranded travelers. Green Corridor still active. Oman continues as primary evacuation hub.
sections.analysis: Oman route remains viable and is the primary evacuation corridor. No credible reports of border closure or major delays beyond the ~1hr queue NYT documented on Mar 9. Oman Air cancellations limit flight options from Muscat but other carriers operating. Route: Dubai → Al Ain → Buraimi/Hili border → Muscat.

sections.title: Civilian & Infrastructure Escalation
sections.summary: 12 dead. 203 injuries (+12 today, WAR RECORD single-day jump). Injuries crossed 200. Multi-weapon attacks correlate with higher casualty rates. Grieco's saturation thesis partially materializing.
sections.analysis: Civilian risk is lower than it was in Week 1. Deaths have plateaued, attack volume is down 92%, and Iran has not deliberately targeted populated areas. The port warning rhetoric is real but hasn't translated into action. Key risk remains debris from intercepts during any volume spike.

sections.title: Market-Implied Conflict Duration
sections.summary: Brent crude $112 (Apr 3). Dated Brent physical oil hit $140, highest since 2008 (Bloomberg Apr 2). Oil surging back from $99 ceasefire-hope dip. Ceasefire talks collapsed: Iran rejected 48hr ceasefire proposal (Reuters). Iran rejected 15-point plan. Pakistan-led mediation hit dead end (Times of Israel). Hotel occupancy still single digits. Tourism losing $600M/day.
sections.analysis: Oil markets are pricing in a prolonged conflict. The IEA SPR release — largest in 50 years — hasn't capped prices because the Hormuz shortfall (~9M bpd) dwarfs the release rate (~1.4M bpd). This is the strongest objective indicator that markets expect the war to continue. Gold retreating suggests some war premium unwinding but oil tells the real story.

sections.title: Conflict Endgame — Is Tehran Still Coherent?
sections.summary: Ceasefire dead again. Iran rejected US 48-hour ceasefire proposal (Reuters/Fars). Iran rejected 15-point peace plan. Pakistan-led regional mediation hit dead end. Iran's 5 conditions: end all attacks, prevent war resumption, compensation, sovereignty over Hormuz. Parliament speaker Ghalibaf: 'Iran cannot be forced into submission.' Trump postponed power plant strikes 5 days for 'productive negotiations,' but Iran denies talks are happening.

metrics.label: Kharg Island
metrics.value: Military targets struck, oil spared (for now)

sources.name: Times of Israel: mediation dead end

sections.analysis: Both sides have explicitly rejected ceasefire through tier-1 outlets. This is not inference — it's direct statements. The gap between positions is wide: Trump wants nuclear surrender, Iran wants strikes to stop first. No mediator has bridged this. Kharg Island strike puts Iran's economic lifeline at risk, which hardens Iran's position rather than softening it. The war is not ending this week.

red.note: Day 11: Drone intercept at 74.3% — well below 85% threshold. 9 hit UAE territory.

red.trigger: Cruise missile wave targeting UAE cities
red.note: Day 12: 7 cruise missiles in single day. Cumulative now 15 (was 8 for first 11 days).
Bahrain desal hit — UAE not yet
3 drone/debris incidents near DXB. Latest Mar 11: 2 drones, 4 injured. Ops continue.

amber.note: No confirmed deliberate targeting — all residential damage is interception debris
Resumed limited schedule Mar 9 after Mar 7 suspension

amber.trigger: Second UAE fatality from direct hit
Iran deploys cruise missiles at Gulf cities

green.note: Trump: 'war very complete' — first US de-escalation signal. No formal ceasefire yet.
